formation_under_examination: Plymouth Brethren-adjacent: classical Reformed Protestant orthodoxy plus Brethren distinctives. This formation is the position under examination, NOT the rubric. The inferred-baseline run tests whether each proposition holds against apparatus + interlinear + concordance, with counter-witness traditions corroborating the lexical reading.
description: Universal question bank: statement, anchors, tier, consensus, brethren-distinctive flag. Combines with the answer file (baseline.json or responses/<id>.json or consolidated.json) to render the personal beliefs PDF; stands alone to render the blank questionnaire. The 222 question entries are scheduled for a phase-3 reframe pass (verdict-pre-loading audit + overlap disambiguation); see docs/QUESTION_SCHEMA.md.

tier_definitions.essential: Core gospel and Trinitarian boundaries. Denial = false gospel or cult-grade error. Always teach to children, always correct, never become member where denied.
tier_definitions.convictional: Clear scriptural conviction. Membership-level threshold. Would not compromise on doctrine, but visit/participate is usually fine where denied.
tier_definitions.important: Matters significantly but does not break fellowship. Reservations about membership, but not absolute. Marry across is usually fine.
tier_definitions.preference: Methodological or practical. Membership across is fine. Worship style, communion frequency, calendar customs.
tier_definitions.adiaphora: Truly indifferent. Freedom of conscience. All thresholds permissive.

field_definitions.id: Stable kebab-case slug. Prefix doc- for doctrine, prc- for practice, cult- for cult marker, het- for heterodoxy marker.
field_definitions.subcategory: Optional finer grouping; null if not applicable.
field_definitions.statement: The proposition under examination. Phase-3 target: neutral interrogative-equivalent proposition, NOT a verdict assertion. Existing 222 entries inherited a 'phrased as the orthodox/affirming claim so denial is clear' rule that pre-loads verdicts and uses Reformed-confessional vocabulary as if neutral; the inferred-baseline run flags such cases via evidence.stem_audit and a phase-3 rewrite pass produces neutral-form replacements. See docs/QUESTION_SCHEMA.md.
field_definitions.scripture_anchors: Seed biblical passages relevant to the proposition. Inferred-baseline subagents extend these via concordance traversal (every Strong's lemma in the seed verses spider-mapped to the canon); the anchors are a starting point, not a closed list.
field_definitions.confessional_anchors: Creeds/confessions historically addressing this proposition. Recorded as INFORMATIONAL research aids during the inferred run (under evidence.counter_witness with the appropriate tradition tag), NOT as authority. Confessions never settle a verdict; apparatus + interlinear + concordance do. See tools/derive_baseline_prompt.md.
field_definitions.tier: essential = Nicene-Chalcedonian-Apostolic foundations under cross-tradition consensus (denial = false gospel) | convictional = clear from apparatus + concordance with legitimate intramural dispute | important = matters but does not break fellowship | preference = methodological/practical | adiaphora = indifferent, freedom of conscience.
field_definitions.historical_consensus: orthodox = cross-tradition consensus across patristic + magisterial + Lutheran + Anglican + Reformed + Methodist + Anabaptist + Pentecostal lineages | divided = legitimate intramural disagreement | minority = real but smaller stream | heterodox = outside historic consensus, not formally cult | heretical = formally condemned in early ecumenical councils.
field_definitions.brethren_distinctive: true if this position is distinctive to the Brethren tradition (vs. broadly held by orthodox Christianity). Marker for when a verdict aligns with a brethren-distinctive — not a disqualifier, but a sign the verdict requires extra counter-witness scrutiny.

questions.statement: The biblical canon is closed at the close of the apostolic age, and no later writing or oral revelation given after the apostolic age stands on par with Scripture or binds the conscience as Scripture does.
Scripture is the final and supreme authority for the Church, above all tradition, councils, and personal experience.
Scripture in the original autographs is without error in all that it affirms, including its statements about history, science, and doctrine.
Scripture cannot fail to accomplish what God intends and is incapable of misleading in matters of faith and practice.
All Scripture is verbally and plenarily inspired by God, such that the very words of the original autographs are God-breathed.
Scripture is sufficient for faith, doctrine, and practice; no further revelation is needed for salvation or godliness.

The biblical canon comprises the 39 Old Testament and 27 New Testament books recognized by the Protestant tradition; the Apocryphal/Deuterocanonical books are not Scripture and not authoritative for doctrine, though they may have historical or edifying value.
Scripture is sufficiently clear in what it teaches concerning salvation that an ordinary believer reading with the Spirit's help can grasp those teachings without specialized mediation.
God is utterly holy: set apart in moral purity and majestic transcendence.
God is love in His very essence, eternally loving within the Trinity and toward His creatures.
God is all-powerful, able to do all that He wills, limited only by His own nature.
God knows all things — actual and possible, past, present, and future — exhaustively and eternally, including all future free human actions.
God sovereignly rules over all things, working all events according to the counsel of His will.
The one true and living God exists eternally as the self-existent creator and sustainer of all things.
The Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are three distinct, coeternal Persons sharing one divine essence — distinct in personhood, not three modes or roles successively assumed by one Person.
There is one God eternally existing in three coequal, coeternal, distinct Persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
The three Persons of the Trinity share one divine essence; they are not three separate gods.
God is self-existent, deriving His being from nothing outside Himself.
God is unchanging in His being, perfections, purposes, and promises.
God is fully present everywhere, not bounded by space, while remaining distinct from creation.
God eternally decrees whatsoever comes to pass, in a way consistent with creaturely responsibility.
God is not composed of parts; His attributes are identical with His essence.
Christ ascended bodily into heaven forty days after His resurrection.
Jesus Christ rose bodily from the dead in the same physical body that was crucified, now glorified — not as a merely spiritual or non-physical resurrection.
Jesus Christ is fully and uniquely God, eternally begotten and not created, equal in essence with the Father.
Jesus Christ is fully and truly human, taking on a complete human nature including body and rational soul.
Jesus Christ was without sin in His thoughts, words, and deeds throughout His earthly life.
Christ is the sole mediator between God and humanity (1 Tim 2:5; Heb 7:25; John 14:6); access to God is not mediated by any other figure.
The Son is eternally the Son (co-eternal and co-equal with the Father), not adopted or begun in time.
Christ is one person in two natures (fully divine and fully human), without confusion, change, division, or separation.
Christ died as a substitute, bearing the penalty for the sins of His people in their place.
Jesus Christ was conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the virgin Mary.
The risen Christ is exalted at the right hand of the Father, reigning and interceding for His people.
In the incarnation Christ took on a true human nature without surrendering or giving up the divine attributes proper to His deity.
Christ possesses two wills (divine and human) corresponding to His two natures, working in harmony.
The Holy Spirit is fully and truly God, co-equal and co-eternal with the Father and the Son.
The Holy Spirit indwells every true believer permanently from the moment of regeneration.
The Holy Spirit is a personal being (possessing intellect, will, and emotion), not an impersonal force or energy.
The Holy Spirit is the agent of regeneration, granting new birth to those dead in sin.
Blasphemy against the Holy Spirit (final, hardened rejection of His witness to Christ) is unforgivable.
The Spirit baptizes every believer into the body of Christ at conversion — a once-for-all initiation into Christ rather than a separable, subsequent crisis experience.
The Holy Spirit illumines Scripture to believers, enabling spiritual understanding of God's word.
The Holy Spirit seals every believer as God's possession, guaranteeing their final inheritance.
The Holy Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father (Nicene-Constantinopolitan confession of 381).
The Holy Spirit also eternally proceeds from the Son (filioque, per the Western tradition).
Adam and Eve were real historical persons, the first human pair, from whom all humanity descends.
Adam and Eve were specially created by God and are not the product of unguided evolutionary processes.
God created humanity male and female as a fixed binary rooted in creation; biological sex and gender identity are coherent and not subject to self-determined redefinition.

All human beings are created in the image of God (imago Dei), bearing inherent dignity, moral agency, rationality, and capacity for relationship with God, irrespective of age, ability, ethnicity, or stage of development.
Human beings are a unity of material body and immaterial soul/spirit; whether constitution is dichotomous (body+soul) or trichotomous (body+soul+spirit) is a debated but in-house question.
The Fall was a real historical event in which Adam, as covenant head, disobeyed God, plunging humanity and the created order into sin, death, and corruption.
Fallen humans, apart from the Spirit's prior work, are unable to please God, repent, or believe savingly; the natural man cannot receive the things of the Spirit.
All human beings are born with a sinful nature inherited from Adam, sharing in his corruption, and are by nature objects of divine wrath.
Adam's guilt, not only his corruption, is reckoned to all his natural descendants such that humanity stands legally guilty in him from conception.

Sin is any want of conformity to, or transgression of, the law of God (in thought, word, deed, and disposition) and is fundamentally an offense against God's holy character, not merely against neighbour.
Sin has corrupted every faculty of fallen man (mind, will, affections, body) such that no part is untouched and unaided man cannot save himself or initiate saving faith.
Conscience is a divinely-given internal witness to God's law that, though damaged by sin, accuses or excuses moral agents and renders all without excuse before God.
Adam acted as the covenant/federal head of humanity, so that his sin and its consequences are imputed to all his natural descendants, paralleling Christ as federal head of the redeemed.
Idolatry (exchanging the glory of the Creator for created things) is the root of all sin; every transgression is a derivative form of misplaced worship.
At Christ's return, believers will be raised bodily, transformed, and glorified: the final consummation of salvation in resurrection bodies free from sin and decay.

In justification, Christ's righteousness — both His perfect obedience and His vicarious endurance of the law's penalty — is reckoned to the believer's account, so that the believer is accounted righteous before God on the basis of what Christ has done, not on the basis of any righteousness inherent in the believer.

Justification is God's declarative act in which He pardons the sinner and accepts him as righteous — not on the ground of any change wrought within the sinner, but on the ground of Christ's righteousness received by faith.
Humans are not born morally neutral — they enter the world under Adam's fall, unable to choose God or obey His law from natural ability alone, prior to grace.

Christ's atoning death includes the bearing of God's just judgment against sin in the place of sinners — Christ taking on Himself the penalty their sins deserved, so that God's justice against sin is satisfied at the cross.
Salvation is through Christ alone; no other religion or path provides saving access to God.
Saving access to Christ's work requires conscious, explicit faith in the historical Jesus; saving faith is not granted apart from such explicit response to the gospel.
The gospel is the good news of forgiveness of sins through Christ's death and resurrection; God does not guarantee material wealth or physical health in this life as a fruit of saving faith.
Repentance (a Spirit-given change of mind toward sin and toward God, issuing in turning from sin to Christ) is integral to saving conversion, never separable from saving faith.

Justification is received by faith alone, apart from works of the law as a meritorious ground; works are the necessary fruit, never the basis, of justification, and any system that makes human merit, ritual observance, law-keeping, or moral effort the partial or whole ground of justification before God denies what Scripture says is the basis of justification.
Salvation is by grace alone — wholly God's unmerited favor, never earned, contributed to, or co-purchased by human effort, ritual, ethnic descent, or moral merit.
Salvation is in Christ alone — there is no other name under heaven by which men must be saved, no other mediator between God and man.
Christ died for sinners in their place — His death is, among other things, a substitute death, by which those for whom He died are reconciled to God.
By adoption, those justified are received into the family of God, granted the privileges, name, and inheritance of sons through union with Christ and indwelling of the Spirit of adoption.

Grace empowers the believer to walk in holiness; the moral law continues to function as the rule of the believer's life — not as the ground of justification, but as the contour of the obedience to which the believer is called.
confessional_anchors: Heidelberg Catechism (third use of the law)

questions.statement: Conversion is the conscious human response to the gospel (repentance and faith) by which the regenerated sinner turns from sin to Christ; both gift of God and act of the believer.
Adding human rules to God's commands as conditions of acceptance, fellowship, or sanctification — and treating obedience as the ground rather than the fruit of grace — distorts the gospel.
Those truly born again will be kept by the power of God through faith unto final salvation; genuine believers cannot ultimately and finally fall away.

Sanctification is the progressive, Spirit-wrought work of conforming believers to the image of Christ; distinct from justification but inseparable from it; engaged through means of grace and Spirit-empowered effort.
Regeneration (the new birth) is a monergistic work of the Holy Spirit, sovereignly imparting spiritual life to those dead in sin; logically prior to and the cause of saving faith.
The sinner does not take the first step toward God by natural ability with grace then assisting; rather, the prior gracious work of God enables any movement of the will toward Him.
Believers may have well-grounded assurance of salvation, founded on the promises of God in Christ, the inward witness of the Spirit, and the evidence of Spirit-wrought fruit.
Salvation is effected by Spirit-wrought new birth received through faith, not by an outward act of decision (raised hand, walked aisle, repeated prayer) treated as itself the saving event.
God calls His people through the Word and Spirit: outwardly through preaching and inwardly so that the call is effective unto salvation in those who respond by faith.

Before the foundation of the world, God chose a people for salvation in Christ; whether election is unconditional (Calvinist) or based on foreseen faith (classical Arminian/Brethren) is a divided question.

The atonement is sufficient for all but applied/efficient for the elect (or, in some traditions, genuinely universal in provision); Brethren tradition typically holds general atonement, with sincere universal gospel offer.
God ordinarily nourishes faith and sanctifies His people through appointed means: the Word read and preached, prayer, the breaking of bread, baptism, and Spirit-empowered Christian fellowship.

The 'sinner's prayer' as a evangelistic tool is permissible if it expresses genuine repentance and faith, but is dangerous when treated as a saving formula or basis for assurance apart from ongoing fruit.
Christ alone is the Head of the Church; no human being holds that office or shares that authority.
The Church is the body of Christ, a spiritual organism composed of all true believers from Pentecost to the rapture, united to Christ by the indwelling Holy Spirit.
confessional_anchors: Nicene Creed (one holy catholic apostolic Church)

questions.statement: Each local assembly is autonomous under Christ's headship, governed by its own elders, not subordinate to a denominational hierarchy or external bishop.
A true visible church is marked by: (1) the Word of God rightly preached, (2) the ordinances rightly administered, (3) church discipline rightly exercised.
Scripture knows no two-tier clergy/laity distinction; all believers are saints and ministers, with elders functioning as servant-overseers, not a priestly class.
confessional_anchors: Brethren tradition (no formal confession; embodied in assembly practice)

questions.statement: The Church is one (united in Christ), holy (set apart, indwelt by the Spirit), catholic (universal across time and place), and apostolic (built on the apostles' teaching, not on apostolic succession of office).
Every believer is a priest before God through Christ; there is no mediatorial priesthood between Christ and the believer beyond Christ Himself.
The Church exists in two senses: universal (all believers in all times and places) and local (particular gatherings of believers in covenanted fellowship).
Baptism is the outward sign of an inward reality accomplished by grace through faith, and does not in itself effect regeneration or wash away sin apart from the inner work of the Spirit and faith.
The Lord's Supper is primarily a memorial of Christ's once-for-all atoning death; 'do this in remembrance of me.'
The Lord's Supper includes spiritual communion with the risen Christ by faith; believers feed on Christ spiritually, distinguishing the position from bare Zwinglian memorialism.

The covenant-of-grace argument that the New Covenant sign of baptism replaces the Old Covenant sign of circumcision and therefore extends to the children of believers fails to override the consistent New Testament pattern of faith preceding baptism.

The bread and wine in the Lord's Supper are not, in substance, changed into Christ's body and blood at the words of institution; the elements remain bread and wine while signifying and (under some accounts) communicating Christ's body and blood spiritually.
Christ instituted two ordinances for the Church — baptism and the Lord's Supper — that signify and seal the gospel.
Baptism is for professing believers only, not for infants; faith precedes baptism in the New Testament pattern.
Baptism is administered by full immersion as a public confession of faith and identification with Christ's death, burial, and resurrection.

Real-presence language preserved by the Lutheran "in, with, and under" formula avoids transubstantiation but requires a doctrine of the ubiquity of Christ's glorified body that creates Christological tensions of its own.

Of the seven rites the Catholic and Orthodox traditions identify as sacraments (baptism, confirmation, eucharist, penance, anointing of the sick, holy orders, matrimony), only baptism and the Lord's Supper are explicitly instituted by Christ as ordinances of the Church.
Foot-washing (John 13) is a beautiful pattern of servant love but was not instituted by Christ as an ongoing ordinance for the Church alongside baptism and the Supper.
Communion should be open to all believers in Christ who are walking in fellowship with the Lord and not under church discipline, regardless of denominational affiliation or specific baptismal mode.
The Lord's Supper (breaking of bread) is observed weekly on the Lord's Day as the central act of corporate worship.
Apostolic continuity in the Church is grounded in fidelity to the apostles' teaching rather than in the unbroken transmission of episcopal office through the laying-on of hands.

The New Testament uses "bishop / overseer" and "elder / presbyter" interchangeably for one office; it does not establish a tier of bishops with authority over multiple congregations distinct from elders.
No human bishop or council possesses supreme authority over the universal Church; no human teacher pronounces with the infallibility that belongs to Scripture alone as the rule of faith and life.

Each local assembly should be led by a plurality of qualified elders (overseers/shepherds) shepherding jointly, not by a single senior pastor or CEO-style sole leader; concentrating authority and teaching ministry in one man departs from the apostolic plurality pattern. Watch for elder boards that exist only nominally and rubber-stamp a CEO-pastor.
The office of elder/overseer is reserved for qualified men; women are not to hold the eldership/teaching-pastor office over mixed adult assemblies.
Congregational polity (final earthly authority resting in the gathered congregation under Christ, with elders leading and the assembly affirming) best preserves NT autonomy and accountability.
Deacons are a NT office of servant-ministers handling practical care and administration in the local assembly, distinct from the teaching/oversight role of elders.

Formal ordination as conferral of sacerdotal status or unique ministerial grace is rejected; recognition of elders/deacons by the local assembly with prayer and laying on of hands is biblical, but creates no clergy class.

Presbyterian polity (graded courts of teaching and ruling elders: session, presbytery, general assembly) has biblical merit (Acts 15) but tends toward over-institutionalisation and erodes local-assembly autonomy.

Women may serve in significant diaconal, hospitality, mercy, evangelism, women's ministry, children's ministry, missionary, and helping roles; gifting and calling are not gender-restricted in non-authoritative contexts.
Both bivocational eldership (tentmaking) and full-time supported ministry are biblically valid; neither is the only legitimate form, and Brethren tradition has favoured bivocational/lay eldership.
Formal seminary or theological-college credentials are not biblically required for elder qualification; the NT criteria are character, doctrinal soundness, and ability to teach (1 Tim 3, Titus 1).
Small to mid-sized assemblies (sub-Dunbar) where members are known by name and elders can shepherd individually are preferable to mega-church structures.
Church discipline follows the Matthew 18 progression (private confrontation, small-group confrontation, assembly confrontation, removal) and is exercised in love for repentance and restoration.
Excommunication (removal from fellowship and the Lord's Table) is the final step of the Matthew 18 process for unrepentant sin, intended to provoke repentance and protect the assembly.
When a disciplined member shows credible repentance, the assembly should restore them publicly, forgiving and reaffirming love.
Formal church membership (public covenanted commitment between believer and local assembly under elder oversight) is biblically warranted and prudentially essential for accountability and discipline.

Women are not to take up authoritative speaking ministry in the gathered mixed assembly (sermon preaching, doctrinal teaching of mixed adults, and leading the assembly authoritatively per 1 Cor 14:34-35; 1 Tim 2:11-12); women may pray, sing, prophesy in non-authoritative contexts, share testimony, teach women and children, and minister widely otherwise.

Paul's instruction in 1 Corinthians 11 about women covering their heads during prayer and prophecy in the gathered assembly continues to apply; the head-covering is a sign of the creation-order distinction Paul names there, applicable in the gathered assembly today (whether by literal covering or by another culturally-coherent sign of that distinction).
Both liturgical (set forms) and spontaneous (Spirit-led) worship are biblically defensible; what is done in worship should have scriptural warrant, but the form-spectrum is wide.

A meeting in which qualified brothers may, as the Spirit leads, contribute audibly (reading, praying, giving thanks, suggesting a hymn, sharing a word) reflects the participatory pattern Paul describes in 1 Corinthians 14:26-33.
Groups that deny the Trinity are not Christian in the historical-creedal sense, and the Church's posture toward such groups is evangelism rather than ecumenical fellowship-union.

Formal ecumenical union on shared sacramental, ministerial, and gospel ground is barred by substantive disagreement over justification, the Mass as sacrifice, papal infallibility, and Marian dogmas; until those disagreements are resolved, ecumenical union is not a matter of mere structural convergence.

Denominationalism (the proliferation of human party-labels: Lutheran, Methodist, etc.) is a regrettable historical reality, but recognition of distinct gospel-faithful traditions is realistic; Brethren conviction is to gather simply on the name of Christ without sectarian label.
Parachurch ministries (mission agencies, campus ministries, mercy organisations, training institutions) are legitimate when they support and serve the local church rather than replace it.
There will be a bodily resurrection of both the just and the unjust at the end of the age.
Unbelievers face eternal, conscious punishment in hell as the just judgment of a holy God.
Christ will judge all people at his return: believers to eternal life, unbelievers to eternal punishment.

Jesus Christ will return personally, visibly, audibly, and bodily in a yet-future event to judge the living and the dead — not as a past event already fulfilled in AD 70 nor as an exclusively spiritual reality without bodily appearing.
Not all people will ultimately be saved; some are excluded from saving fellowship with God in the final judgment.
The wicked are not ultimately destroyed and reduced to non-existence; the punishment of the unjust in the final state is conscious and ongoing.
Christ's return is imminent and could occur at any time, but no one knows the day or the hour, and date-setting is forbidden.
Believers who die are immediately and consciously present with the Lord, at rest, awaiting bodily resurrection. Soul sleep (unconsciousness of the dead until resurrection) is rejected.
God will create new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells, the eternal home of the redeemed.
Believers who die in Christ are immediately and finally purified in His finished work, not progressively purified in a post-mortem state of purgation prior to glory.
There is a future, visible salvation of ethnic Israel as a people when 'all Israel will be saved' (Rom 11:26).
Prayers offered on behalf of the dead — whether to alter their post-mortem state or to commend them to God — lack biblical warrant; the finality of judgment after death is settled at death.
The thousand-year reign of Revelation 20 is a symbolic representation of the present church age between Christ's two advents (amillennialism).
Scripture distinguishes God's program with ethnic Israel and God's program with the Church as distinct programs, not one continuous redemptive program across the testaments.
Christ returns after — not before — an extended age of gospel triumph in which the kingdom progressively transforms the world (postmillennialism).
The Church is gathered to Christ at His visible return after the tribulation — there is no separate, prior secret rapture before the tribulation begins.
Christ returns before a literal thousand-year reign on earth (premillennialism), as a future, post-second-coming era distinct from the present age.
Christ removes the Church from the earth in a separate event before a seven-year tribulation period, distinct from and prior to His visible second coming (pre-tribulation rapture).
Angels exist as personal, created, ministering spirits who serve God and his elect.
A portion of the angels fell into rebellion under Satan and are now demons opposed to God.
Guardian angels (angels assigned to particular believers) is a permissible but not dogmatically required position.
Satan is a real, personal, created spiritual being who opposes God and his people.
A genuine believer, indwelt by the Holy Spirit, cannot be possessed (inhabited) by a demon, though believers can be oppressed, tempted, and harassed.
Deliverance ministry is biblically warranted in genuine cases of demonic possession but is open to abuse and requires great caution.
Unbelievers can be subject to demonic possession (full inhabitation), as the Gospels and Acts repeatedly attest.
Sin, sickness, suffering, and physical reality are real; salvation is from real sin to real eternal life through Christ's real death and resurrection.

Saving union with Christ is received through faith and is not effected at the moment of water baptism such that baptism is the instrument of regeneration; and shepherding relationships in the Church do not require the disciple's submission of every life decision to a designated leader.
confessional_anchors: all Reformation confessions on sola fide

questions.statement: There is one and only one true God, eternally and unchangeably; humans do not become gods, and the Son of God is not one among multiple divine beings. Jesus Christ is the eternal, uncreated Son of one substance with the Father, not a created spiritual being elder-brother to Satan or to humanity.
Modern Anglo-Saxon, European, or any other modern ethnic group is not biblically identified with the lost tribes of Israel; the Church is not coextensive with any one race or nation.
The honor due to God alone is not to be rendered to images of Christ, of saints, or of the cross; saints are not to be petitioned in prayer as mediators of access to God.

Mary, the mother of Jesus, is herself among those redeemed by Christ — she was not preserved from original sin at her own conception, was not bodily assumed at the end of her life, and does not co-mediate the redemption Christ accomplished.
The gospel is to be freely offered to all people; God commands all to repent and believe; the well-meant offer of the gospel is not contingent on the hearer's elect status.
Physical healing is guaranteed in the atonement for every believer in this life (such that lack of healing implies lack of faith).

Phenomena such as falling under the Spirit treated as normative, sustained ecstatic laughter, prophetic decrees claimed to bind the conscience, and physically dramatic signs treated as the standard of Spirit-presence are not the New Testament pattern of orderly worship.

The office of apostle in the foundational, Scripture-authoring sense — eyewitness of the risen Christ, sent with Christ's authority to lay the Church's foundation — was filled by the apostles of Christ and is not continuing today; claims to apostolic *office* in this sense after the apostolic age do not bind the Church's conscience, though missionary church-planting work that is sometimes called "apostolic" in a non-office sense is unaffected.
Any modern claim to prophecy must be tested by Scripture and is never binding on conscience the way Scripture is. New revelation that adds to or contradicts Scripture is rejected.
Speaking in tongues is the necessary evidence of Spirit baptism and/or genuine salvation (classical Pentecostal initial-evidence doctrine).

Human speech does not possess intrinsic creative force such that "decreeing and declaring" causes God to bring about the speaker's stated outcome; the Creator's word and the creature's word are categorically distinct.
The miraculous sign gifts (apostleship, prophecy, tongues, miraculous healings) ceased with the apostolic age and the closing of the canon.
Demonic activity is real and Christians may need pastoral care including prayer for deliverance, but routinized 'deliverance ministry' that locates a demon behind every sin and trauma is unbalanced.
God can and sometimes does heal in answer to prayer; healing is a sovereign mercy, not a guaranteed outcome.
The biblical gift of tongues, as exhibited at Pentecost, is the Spirit-given ability to speak real human languages previously unknown to the speaker, for the sake of intelligible witness.
Worship design that systematically downplays Scripture, sin, judgment, or the cross to make the service more accessible to unbelievers reorients worship away from its God-ward end.
Christian worship rightly centers on the public reading and preaching of Scripture, alongside prayer, song, and the Lord's Supper.
Congregational singing (the people of God lifting voices together) is the primary musical mode of Christian worship.
Instrumentation in worship is a matter of Christian liberty; simple instrumentation or a cappella worship is honored historically in Brethren practice but not binding on others.
A formal liturgy or lectionary is not commanded but is a permissible Christian-liberty matter when it preserves the gospel and Scripture's primacy.
Singing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs (including doctrinally rich historic hymns and biblically grounded modern songs) is good and commanded.

Human life bears the image of God from conception to natural death and is to be protected; the intentional taking of innocent unborn human life is sin under the sixth commandment, not a morally neutral matter or a matter of Christian liberty.
Christians submit to civil authority as God's servant, but must obey God rather than man when laws command sin or forbid commanded obedience.
Active euthanasia and assisted suicide are wrong; allowing natural death and refusing futile treatment is permitted.
Truth-telling, integrity in word, and restitution where wrong has been done are required Christian practice.
Just-war and pacifist positions are both held by faithful Christians; Brethren tradition has historically leaned toward pacifism or non-combatant service while not making it absolute.
Tobacco use is a matter of Christian liberty with health-stewardship cautions, not sin per se.
Recreational drug use, including illegal drugs and intoxication-aimed cannabis, is sin and lawbreaking.
Alcohol consumption in moderation is a matter of Christian liberty; drunkenness is sin. Brethren tradition often counsels abstinence or strict moderation.
Capital punishment for the most serious crimes, administered by lawful civil authority with due process, is permissible (open, with prudential cautions).
Gambling for gain at another's loss is unwise and contrary to neighbor-love; it is to be avoided as a regular practice.
God created humanity male and female; biological sex is binary, given by God, and immutable. Transgender ideology (that gender identity supersedes biological sex) is to be rejected.
Marriage is a covenant union of one man and one woman, instituted by God at creation, intended for life, picturing Christ and the church.
Same-sex sexual activity is sin, contrary to creation design and the consistent witness of Scripture.

Marriage is intended for life; divorce is permitted only in cases of sexual immorality (Matt 19) and abandonment by an unbelieving spouse (1 Cor 7); remarriage after such biblically permitted divorce is permissible.
Pornography use is sin: adultery of the heart, exploitation of those in the industry, and corrosive of the marriage bed.
Sexual intimacy is reserved for marriage between one man and one woman; pre-marital sex (fornication) and unmarried cohabitation that includes sexual relations are sin.
Singleness and celibacy are honorable callings, gifts from God, equally valued with marriage.
IVF and reproductive technologies are open with serious ethical concerns about embryo destruction, selection, and surrogacy detached from marriage.
Non-abortifacient contraception is a matter of Christian liberty; abortifacient methods (those that prevent implantation of a fertilized egg) are inconsistent with sanctity-of-life ethics.
Modesty in dress and self-presentation, without legalism, is a Christian virtue rooted in love of neighbor and godly self-restraint.

Complementarianism: men and women are equal in worth and dignity, with distinct, complementary roles; the offices of elder/overseer and the function of authoritative church teaching are reserved for qualified men, husbands have headship and wives respond with willing submission in mutual love and Christ-likeness. Egalitarianism (that all church offices and authoritative-teaching functions are open to women and that no role distinction obtains in marriage) is not the position of this baseline.
Fathers bear primary responsibility for the spiritual formation of their children, with mothers; family worship and household catechesis are normal Christian practice.
Loving, measured corporal discipline of young children is permitted by Scripture; abusive practice is sinful and unlawful. Cultural and legal contexts shape application.
Church finances should be handled with integrity, transparency, plural oversight, and openness to the congregation.

Christians are called to generous, proportional, cheerful giving from a willing heart; the mandatory 10% tithe of the Mosaic covenant is not directly binding on the church, though tithing is a useful baseline.
The church is to financially support its elders who labor in the word and its missionaries sent out for the gospel.

Biblical justice (care for the poor, the orphan, the widow, the oppressed) is a Christian duty; but ideological 'social justice' that supplants the gospel of forgiveness in Christ with group-based justice is a different gospel.

Christian nationalism (the fusion of Christian identity with national identity such that the nation-state is treated as a vehicle of Christ's kingdom) is to be regarded with caution and largely rejected.
Persecution and suffering for Christ are normal, expected aspects of Christian discipleship in this age.
Caring for creation as God's stewards is a Christian duty, distinct from creation-worship or pantheist environmentalism.
Christians are free to engage politically (voting, advocacy, public service) as a form of neighbor-love, while the church's mission is the gospel and not partisan alignment.
Public schooling, Christian schooling, and homeschooling are all permissible Christian approaches; parental responsibility for discipleship remains primary regardless.
The first day of the week, the Lord's Day, is the appointed Christian gathering day in honor of Christ's resurrection.

The Saturday Sabbath of the Mosaic calendar is not binding on Christians as a day-specific observance; the moral principle of one-in-seven rest endures, but the New Testament does not require believers to observe the seventh-day Sabbath.

The observance of Christmas and Easter (Resurrection Sunday) is a matter of Christian liberty (Romans 14:5-6); the Christian calendar is not commanded in the New Testament but neither is its observance forbidden.
Active participation in occult or death-glorifying aspects of Halloween is rejected; harmless cultural participation (costumes, neighbor-friendly engagement) is a matter of conscience.

Lent, Ash Wednesday, and saint-days are not commanded by Scripture as observances binding on the Christian conscience; personal fasting and seasonal reflection remain commendable Christian disciplines apart from a calendar treated as obligatory.

Eve's transgression in Eden was disobedience to God's command, not sexual congress with the serpent or with Satan; Cain is the natural son of Adam and Eve; all of humanity descends from one originating pair, not from divergent racial lines traceable to Satan.

Shepherding relationships in the Church do not require the disciple's unconditional submission of every life decision to a designated leader; binding the conscience in matters Scripture leaves free is contrary to the apostolic pattern.

Women may take up non-teaching platform-leadership roles in the gathered mixed assembly (leading congregational singing, leading public prayer, reading Scripture, sharing testimony, making announcements) without exercising authoritative teaching.
